                                                                                                                           8-8

                                                                  portable frame is shown in Figure 8-10. This hoist assembly
          Engine lifting point                                    is specifically manufactured for the purpose of removing
                                                                  engines and other large assemblies from aircraft. Some
                                                                  frames are fitted with power-operated hoists. These should
                                                                  be used with care, since considerable damage can be done
                                                                  if an inexperienced operator allows a power-operated hoist
                                                                  to overrun. The hoist and frame should also be checked for
                                                                  condition before being used to lift the engine.

                                                                  Hoisting the Engine
                                                                  Before the hoist is hooked onto the engine sling, recheck the
                                                                  aircraft tail supports and the wheel chocks. Fasten lines to the
                                                                  engine, at points on the sides or rear, so that the engine can
                                                                  be controlled as it is being hoisted. Hook the hoist onto the
     Engine lifting point
                                                                  sling and hoist the engine just enough to relieve the engine
                                                                  weight from the mount attachments. Remove the nuts from
Figure 8-9. Hoisting sling attached to engine.                    the mount attachments in the order recommended in the
                                                                  manufacturer’s instructions for the aircraft. As the last nuts
a hoisting sling. The location of these attaching points varies   are being removed, pull back on the lines fastened to the
according to the size and weight distribution of the engine.      engine (or force it back by other means if lines are not being
Figure 8-9 shows a sling supporting an engine that has two        used), thus steadying the engine. If bolts must be removed
attaching points. As a matter of safety, carefully inspect the    from the mount attachments, be sure the engine is under
sling for condition before installing it on the engine.           control before doing so. If the bolts are to remain in the mount
                                                                  attachments, the hoist can be gently maneuvered upward or
Before attaching the sling to the hoist, be sure that the hoist   downward as necessary after all the nuts have been removed.
has sufficient capacity to lift the engine safely. The engine’s   Meanwhile, gently relax the backward force on the engine
center of gravity (CG) should also be taken into account as       just enough to allow the engine gradual forward movement
the engine is hoisted. A manually operated hoist mounted in a     when it is free from the mount attachments. When the hoist
                                                                  has removed all engine weight from the mount attachments,
                                                                  the engine should be eased gently forward, away from the
                                                                  aircraft. If the engine binds at any point, maneuver it with
                                                                  the hoist until it slips free.

                                                                  The procedure just discussed applies to removal of most
                                                                  reciprocating and turbine aircraft engines. Any variation
                                                                  in details is outlined in the manufacturer’s instructions.
                                                                  Before attempting any engine removal, always consult these
                                                                  instructions for the aircraft concerned. When the engine has
                                                                  been removed, it can be carefully lowered onto a stand. The
                                                                  engine should be fastened to the stand and prepared for the
                                                                  removal of accessories.

                                                                  Hoisting & Mounting the Engine for Installation
                                                                  When the new or overhauled engine is ready to be hoisted
                                                                  for installation, move the engine stand as close as possible
                                                                  to the nacelle in which the replacement is to be installed.
                                                                  Then, attach the sling to the engine and hook the hoist to the
                                                                  sling. Take up the slack until the hoist is supporting most of
                                                                  the engine weight. Next, remove the engine attaching bolts
                                                                  from the stand and hoist the engine clear.

                                                                  The engine stand may be moved and the hoist frame
Figure 8-10. Hoist and frame assembly used for engine removal.    positioned so the engine can be hoisted easily into the nacelle.

After the engine has been completely installed and connected,
install the propeller on the aircraft. Before doing so, the thrust
bearing retaining nut should be checked for correct torque. If
required, the propeller shaft must be coated with light engine
oil before the propeller is installed; the propeller governor and
anti-icing system must be connected according to applicable
manufacturer’s instructions.

                                                RPM adjustment

                                                                     Disconnect the engine power lever and propeller control rods
                                                                     or cables. Remove the covers from the QECA lift points,
                                                                     attach the QECA sling, and remove slack from the cables
                                                                     using a suitable hoist. The sling must be adjusted to position
                                                                     the hoisting eye over the QECA CG. Failure to do so may
                                                                     result in engine damage.

                                                                     Remove the engine mount bolts. The QECA is then ready to
                                                                     be removed. Recheck all of the disconnect points to make
                                                                     certain they are all disconnected prior to moving the engine.
                                                                     Move the engine forward, out of the nacelle structure, until
                                                                     it clears the aircraft. Lower the QECA into position on the

for each parameter measured. The parameters checked can              The installation procedures are essentially the reverse of

                                                                     Reciprocating Helicopter Engine & QECA
Turboprop Powerplant Removal & Installation
                                                                     The engine is installed facing aft with the propeller shaft
Since most turboprop powerplant removal and installation             approximately 39° above horizontal. The engine is supported
instructions are developed for QECA, the following procedures        by the engine mount, which is bolted to the fuselage structure.
reflect those used for a typical QECA. The procedures for            The installation of the engine provides for ease of maintenance
turboprop engine removal and installation are similar to those       by allowing easy access to all accessories and components
presented in the section of this chapter for turbojet engines,       when the engine access doors are opened. The QECA contains
except for those systems related to the turboprop propeller.         the engine, engine mount, engine accessories, engine controls,
                                                                     fuel system, lubrication system, ignition system, cooling
Open the engine side panels and remove the nacelle access            system, and hydromechanical clutch and fan assembly.
panels. Disconnect the engine thermocouple leads at the
terminal board. Before disconnecting any lines, make sure
that all fuel, oil, and hydraulic fluid valves are closed. Plug

   ASSUME
     1. Ambient temperature: OAT = 12 °F
     2. Barometric pressure = 29.0 inches of mercury

                                                                                                                                    8-16

                                                                   under normal conditions, rubber alone supports the engine.
                                                                   Of course, if the engine is subjected to abnormal shocks or
                                                                   loads, the metal snubbers limit excessive movement of the
                                                                   engine. Dynafocal engine mounts, or vibration isolators, are
                                                                   units that give directional support to the engines. Dynafocal
                                                                   engine mounts have the mounting pad angled to point to the
                                                                   CG of the engines mass. [Figure 8-16]

                                                                   Mounts for Turbofan Engines
                                                                   The engine mounts on most turbofan engines perform the
                                                                   same basic functions of supporting the engine and transmitting
                                                                   the loads imposed by the engine to the aircraft structure. Most
                                                                   turbine engine mounts are made of stainless steel and are
                                                                   typically located as illustrated in Figure 8-17. Some engine
                                                                   mounting systems use two mounts to support the forward end
                                                                   of the engine and a single mount at the rear end.

                                                                   Turbine Vibration Isolation Engine Mounts
                                                                   The vibration isolator engine mounts support the power
                                                                   plants and isolate the airplane structure from adverse
Figure 8-16. Engine mounting ring.                                 engine vibrations. Each power plant is generally supported
                                                                   by forward vibration isolator mounts and an aft vibration
The engine mounts are usually secured to the aircraft by           isolator mount.
special heat-treated steel bolts. The importance of using only
these special bolts can be readily appreciated, since they         The forward vibration isolator engine mounts carry vertical,
alone support the entire weight of, and withstand all, the         side, and axial (thrust) loads and allow engine growth due
stresses imposed by the engine and propeller in flight. The        to thermal expansion. The aft mounts take only vertical and
upper bolts support the weight of the engine while the aircraft    side loads; however, they will also accommodate thermal
is on the ground, but when the aircraft is airborne another        expansion of the engine without applying axial loads to the
stress is added. This stress is torsional and affects all bolts,   engine flanges.
not just the top bolts. A typical engine mount ring shown in
Figure 8-16 discloses fittings and attachment points located
at four positions on the engine mount structure. Each fitting
houses a dynamic engine mount.

The section of an engine mount where the engine is attached
is known as the engine mount ring. It is usually constructed
of steel tubing having a larger diameter than the rest of the
mount structure. It is circular in shape so that it can surround
the engine, which is near the point of balance for the engine.
The engine is usually attached to the mount by dynafocal
mounts, attached to the engine at the point of balance forward
of the mount ring. Other types of mounting devices are also
used to secure the different engines to their mount rings.

As aircraft engines became larger and produced more power,
some method was needed to absorb their vibration. This
demand led to the development of the rubber and steel engine-
suspension units called shock mounts. This combination
permits restricted engine movement in all directions. These
vibration isolators are commonly known as flexible, or elastic,
shock mounts. An interesting feature common to most shock          Figure 8-17. Turbine engine front mount.
mounts is that the rubber and metal parts are arranged so that,

Corrosion-Preventive Materials
An engine in service is in a sense self-purging of moisture,     Although corrosion-preventive compounds act as an insulator
since the heat of combustion evaporates the moisture in and      from moisture, in the presence of excessive moisture, they
around the engine, and the lubricating oil circulated through    eventually break down and corrosion begins. Also, the
the engine temporarily forms a protective coating on the         compounds eventually dry because their oil base gradually
metal it contacts. If the operation of an engine in service      evaporates. This allows moisture to contact the engine’s metal
is limited or suspended for a period of time, the engine is      and aids in corroding it. Therefore, when an engine is stored
preserved to a varying extent, depending upon how long it        in a shipping case or container, some dehydrating (moisture
is to be inoperative. There are three types of engine storage:   removing) agent must be used to remove the moisture from
active engine, temporary, and indefinite. An engine in active    the air in and around the engine.
storage is defined as having at least one continuous hour of
operation with an oil temperature of at least 165 °F to 200 °F   Dehydrating Agents
and storage time not to exceed 30 days. Temporary storage        There are a number of substances (referred to as desiccants)
describes an aircraft and engine that is not flown for 30 to     that can absorb moisture from the atmosphere in sufficient
90 days, and indefinite storage is for an aircraft not to be     quantities to be useful as dehydrators. One of these is silica

Corrosion-Preventive Compounds                                   As a corrosion preventive, bags of silica gel are placed around
The preservation materials discussed are used for all types      and inside various accessible parts of a stored engine. It is
of engine storage. Corrosion-preventive compounds are            also used in clear plastic plugs, called dehydrator plugs,
petroleum-based products that form a wax-like film over the      that can be screwed into engine openings, such as the
metal to which they are applied. Several types of corrosion-     spark plug holes. Cobalt chloride is added to the silica gel
preventive compounds are manufactured according to               used in dehydrator plugs. This additive makes it possible


                                                                                                                            8-18

                                                                 of dehydrator plugs can be dried by removing the silica gel
                                                                 and heating the gel to dry it out, returning it to its original
                                                                 blue color. [Figure 8-19] When the relative humidity is less
                                                                 than 30 percent, corrosion does not normally take place.
                                                                 Therefore, if the dehydrator plugs are bright blue, the air in
                                                                 the engine has so little moisture that internal corrosion is held
                                                                 to a minimum. This same cobalt-chloride-treated silica gel is
                                                                 used in humidity indicator envelopes. These envelopes can
                                                                 be fastened to the stored engine so that they can be inspected
                                                                 through a small window in the shipping case or metal engine
                                                                 container. All desiccants are sealed in containers to prevent
                                                                 their becoming saturated with moisture before they are used.
                                                                 Care should be taken never to leave the container open or
                                                                 improperly closed.

                                                                 Engine Preservation & Return to Service
                                                                 Before an engine is placed in temporary or indefinite storage,
                                                                 it should be operated and filled with a corrosion-preventive oil
                                                                 mixture added in the oil system to retard corrosion by coating
                                                                 the engine’s internal parts. Drain the normal lubricating oil
Figure 8-18. Dehydrator plug “pink” showing high humidity
(Sacramento Sky Ranch).                                          from the sump or system and replace with a preservative oil
                                                                 mixture according to the manufacturer’s instructions. Operate
                                                                 the engine until normal operating temperatures are obtained
                                                                 for at least one hour.

                                                                 Always take the appropriate precautions when turning or
                                                                 working around a propeller. After the flight, remove all the
                                                                 spark plug leads and the top spark plugs.

                                                                 To prevent corrosion, spray each cylinder interior with
                                                                 corrosion-preventive mixture to prevent moisture and oxygen
                                                                 from contacting the deposits left by combustion. Spray the
                                                                 cylinders by inserting the nozzle of the spray gun into each
                                                                 spark plug hole and playing the gun to cover as much area
                                                                 as possible. Before spraying, each cylinder to be treated
                                                                 should be at the bottom center position and the oil at room
                                                                 temperature. This allows the entire inside of the cylinder
                                                                 to become coated with corrosion-preventive mixture. After
                                                                 spraying each engine cylinder at bottom center, respray each
                                                                 cylinder while the crankshaft is stationary with none of the
                                                                 cylinder’s pistons at top dead center.

Preservation & Depreservation of Gas
Turbine Engines
The procedures for preserving and depreserving gas turbine
engines vary depending upon the length of inactivity, the
type of preservative used, and whether or not the engine
may be rotated during the inactive period. Much of the basic
information on corrosion control presented in the section on
reciprocating engines is applicable to gas turbine engines.
However, the requirements for the types of preservatives and
their use are normally different.

The lubrication system is usually drained and may or may
not be flushed with preservative oil. The engine fuel system
is generally filled with preservative oil, including the fuel
control. Before the engine can be returned to service,
the preservative oil must be completely flushed from the
fuel system by motoring the engine and bleeding the fuel
system. Always follow the manufacturer’s instructions
when performing any preservation or depreservation of gas
turbine engines.


                                                                8-22

Chapter 9
Engine Fire Protection Systems
Introduction                                                      much more severe. Penetration of the turbine case by failed
                                                                  blades is a possible fire hazard, as is the penetration of lines
Because fire is one of the most dangerous threats to an
                                                                  and components containing flammable fluids.
aircraft, the potential fire zones of all multiengine aircraft
currently produced are protected by a fixed fire protection
                                                                  A high flow of fuel through an improperly adjusted fuel
system. A “fire zone” is an area or region of an aircraft
                                                                  nozzle can cause burn-through of the tail cone in some
designated by the manufacturer to require fire detection
                                                                  engines. Engine fires can be caused by burning fluid that
and/or fire extinguishing equipment and a high degree
                                                                  occasionally runs out through the exhaust pipe.
of inherent fire resistance. The term “fixed” describes a
permanently installed system in contrast to any type of
                                                                  Components
portable fire extinguishing equipment, such as a hand-held
fire extinguisher.                                                A complete fire protection system includes both a fire
                                                                  detection and a fire extinguishing system. To detect fires
In accordance with Title 14 of the Code of Federal                or overheat conditions, detectors are placed in the various
Regulations (14 CFR) parts 23 and 25, engine fire protection      zones to be monitored. Fires are detected in aircraft by using
systems are mandatory on: multiengine turbine-powered             one or more of the following: overheat detectors, rate-of-
aircraft, multiengine reciprocating engine-powered aircraft       temperature-rise detectors, and flame detectors. In addition
incorporating turbochargers, aircraft with engine(s) located      to these methods, other types of detectors are used in aircraft
where they are not readily visible from the flight deck, all      fire protection systems but are not used to detect engine fires.
commuter and transport category aircraft, and the auxiliary       For example, smoke detectors are better suited to monitor
power unit (APU) compartment of any aircraft incorporating        areas such as baggage compartments or lavatories, where
an APU. Fire protection systems are not mandatory for many        materials burn slowly or smolder. Other types of detectors
single and twin reciprocating engine general aviation (GA)        in this category include carbon monoxide detectors.
aircraft.
                                                                  Fire protection systems on current-production aircraft do not

                                                                         Kidde Continuous-Loop System
                                                                         In the Kidde continuous-loop system, two wires are imbedded
                                                                         in an inconel tube filled with a thermistor core material.
                                                                         [Figure 9-5] Two electrical conductors go through the length
                                                                         of the core. One conductor has a ground connection to the
                                                                         tube and the other conductor connects to the fire detection
                                                                         control unit.

                                                                         As the temperature of the core increases, electrical resistance
                                                                         to ground decreases. The fire detection control unit monitors
                                                                         this resistance. If the resistance decreases to the overheat
                                                                         set point, an overheat indication occurs in the flight deck.
Figure 9-4. Fenwal sensing element.

                                         Sensing element loop      Dual-loop systems automatically perform the loop switching
                                                                   and decision-making function required of the flight crew
                                                                   upon appearance of the fault indication in the flight deck.
                                                                   Automatic self-interrogation eliminates the fault indication
                                                                   and assures the immediate appearance of the fire indication
                                           Test switch
                                                                   should fire occur while at least one loop of the dual-loop
                                          Test circuit             system is operative. Should the control circuit from a single

                  Monitor circuit                                  tests the functioning of the other loop. If it tests operative,
                          Control unit                             the circuit suppresses the fire signal (because the operative
                                                                   loop would have signaled if a fire existed). If, however, the
                                                                   other loop tests inoperative, the circuit outputs a fire signal.

Dual-Loop Systems                                                  Being prebent to the designed configuration assures its
Dual-loop systems are, in essence, two complete basic fire         installation in the aircraft precisely in its designed location,
detection systems with their output signals connected so that      where it has the necessary clearance to be free from the
both must signal to result in a fire warning. This arrangement,    possibility of the elements chafing against engine or aircraft
called “AND” logic, results in greatly increased reliability       structure. The assembly requires only a few attachment
against false fire warnings from any cause. Should one of          points, and removal for engine maintenance is quick and
the two loops be found inoperative at the preflight integrity      easy. Should the assembly require repair or maintenance, it
test, a flight deck selector switch disconnects that loop and      is easily replaced with another assembly, leaving the repair
allows the signal from the other loop alone to activate the        for the shop. A damaged sensing element is easily replaced
fire warning. Since the single operative loop meets all fire       in the assembly. The assembly is rugged, easy to handle, and
detector requirements, the aircraft can be safely dispatched,      unlikely to suffer damage during handling for installation
and maintenance deferred to a more convenient time.                or removal.
However, should one of the two loops become inoperative
in flight and a fire subsequently occur, the fire signaling        Fire Detection Control Unit (Fire Detection Card)

                                                                                                                                        9-9

                                                                Fire Detection System Maintenance
                                                                Fire detector sensing elements are located in many high-
                                                                activity areas around aircraft engines. Their location, together
                                                                with their small size, increases the chance of damage to
                                                                the sensing elements during maintenance. An inspection
                                                                and maintenance program for all types of continuous-loop
                                                                systems should include the following visual checks. These
                                                                procedures are examples and should not be used to replace
                                                                the applicable manufacturer’s instructions.

                                                                Sensing elements of a continuous-loop system should be
                                                                inspected for the following:
Figure 9-11. Discharge indicators.
                                                                  1.   Cracked or broken sections caused by crushing or
                                                                       squeezing between inspection plates, cowl panels, or
                                                                       engine components.
                                                                  2.   Abrasion caused by rubbing of the element on cowling,
                                                                       accessories, or structural members.
                                                                  3.   Pieces of safety wire or other metal particles that may
                                                                       short the spot-detector terminals.
                                                                  4.   Condition of rubber grommets in mounting clamps,
                                                                       which may be softened from exposure to oils or
                                                                       hardened from excessive heat.
                                                                  5.   Dents and kinks in sensing element sections. Limits on
                                                                       the element diameter, acceptable dents and kinks, and
                                                                       degree of smoothness of tubing contour are specified
                                                                       by the manufacturer. No attempt should be made to
                                                                       straighten any acceptable dent or kink, since stresses
                                                                       may be set up that could cause tubing failure. (See
                                                                       illustration of kinked tubing in Figure 9-14.)
                                                                  6.   Nuts at the end of the sensing elements [Figure 9-15]
                                                                       should be inspected for tightness and the presence of
                                                                       a safety wire. Loose nuts should be retorqued to the
                                                                       value specified by the manufacturer’s instructions.
                                                                       Some types of sensing element connection joints
                                                                       require the use of copper crush gaskets, which should
                                                                       be replaced any time a connection is separated.

                                                                  7.   If shielded flexible leads are used, they should be
Figure 9-12. Engine fire switches.
                                                                       inspected for fraying of the outer braid. The braided
                                                                       sheath is composed of many fine metal strands woven
                                                                       into a protective covering surrounding the inner
system malfunctions. [Figure 9-13]
                                                                       insulated wire. Continuous bending of the cable or
                                                                       rough treatment can break these fine wires, especially
Warning Systems
                                                                       those near the connectors.

                                                                   Regular maintenance of fire extinguisher systems typically

Be careful when replacing cartridge and discharge valves.
Most new extinguisher containers are supplied with                                Mounting lugs (4)
their cartridge and discharge valve disassembled. Before
installation on the aircraft, properly assemble the cartridge
in the discharge valve and connect the valve to the container,
usually by means of a swivel nut that tightens against a
packing ring gasket. [Figure 9-19]                                        Pressure gauge

                                                                                   Bottle
If a cartridge is removed from a discharge valve for any
reason, it should not be used in another discharge valve                     Packing ring
assembly, since the distance the contact point protrudes may
vary with each unit. Thus, continuity might not exist if a used
                                                                                                                       Bottle outlet
plug that had been indented with a long contact point were                      Cartridge
installed in a discharge valve with a shorter contact point.

                                                                             Strainer
addressing the principles involved and general procedures to
be followed. When actually performing maintenance, always
refer to the applicable maintenance manuals and other related
                                                                                                        Contact assembly
publications pertaining to a particular aircraft.

Boeing 777 Aircraft Fire Detection &
Extinguishing System
The following section discusses the fire detection and                                                 Swivel nut
extinguishing system of the B777 aircraft. The information
is included only for familiarization purposes.

Each engine has two fire detection loops: loop 1 and loop                                        Discharge plug (or valve) body
2. A fire detection card in the system card file monitors the
loops for fires, overheat conditions, and faults. There is a fire

detection card for each engine.

                                                                                              The engine fire switch has four functions:
                      Safety relief and fill port
                                                                                                    •   Gives an indication of an engine fire,
                                                                        Handle
                                                                                                    •   Stops the engine,

    Mounting lug (4)                                                                                •   Isolates the engine from the aircraft systems, and
                                                                                                    •   Controls the engine fire extinguishing system.

                                                                                              The fire switch assembly incorporates a solenoid that locks
                                                                                              the fire switch so that the flight crew cannot pull it accidently.
                                                                                              If an engine has a fire, the fire warning light comes on and the
    Identification plate                                                                      solenoid energizes to release the switch. When the solenoid
                                                                                              is energized, the fire switch can be pulled.
                                                                              Pressure
                                                                              switch
   Discharge assembly (2)                                                                     When the fire detection system malfunctions or the solenoid
                                                                                              is defective, and the flight crew wants to extinguish an engine
                                                Squib (2)             Pressure switch         fire, someone must push the fire override switch. The fire
                                                                      electrical
                                                                                              override switch allows the fire switch to be pulled when the
                                             Discharge port (2)       connector
                                                                                              solenoid is not energized. When the fire switch is pulled, the
                                   Pressure switch test button                                push-pull switch contacts operate electrical circuits that stop
                                                                                              the engine and isolate it from the aircraft systems. With the
Figure 9-23. Fire extinguishing bottle.                                                       switch pulled, it can be rotated to left or right to a mechanical
                                                                                              stop at the discharge position. The rotary switch contacts
Squib                                                                                         close and operate the fire extinguishing system.
The squib is installed in the discharge assembly at the bottom
of the fire container. A fire container has two squibs, one for                               When the fire switch is pulled, the switch isolates the
each engine. The squib is an electrically operated explosive                                  following aircraft systems from the engine:
device. When the squib is activated, it fires a slug through                                        •   Closes the fuel spar valve.
the breakable disc, and nitrogen pressure inside the bottle                                         •   Deenergizes the engine fuel metering unit (FMU)
pushes the Halon through the discharge port. The squib fires                                            cutoff solenoid.
when the fire switch is pulled and turned to the DISCH 1 or
DISCH 2 position. [Figure 9-22]                                                                     •   Closes the engine hydraulic pump shutoff valve.
                                                                                                    •   Depressurizes the engine driven hydraulic pump valve.
Engine Fire Switches
                                                                                                    •   Closes the pressure regulator and shutoff valve.
The engine fire panel is in the flight deck on the P8 aisle stand.
The engine fire panel has a fire switch for each engine and a                                       •   Removes power from thrust reverser isolation valve.
discharge light for each fire bottle. [Figure 9-26]                                                 •   Trips the generator field.
                                                                                                    •   Trips the backup generator field. [Figure 9-27]

bottle manually. Fire switches are located on the cargo fire/
engine control panel and the service and APU shutdown
panel located outside the aircraft on the nose landing gear.
[Figure 9-28]

APU Fire Warning
If there is an APU fire, the APU fire detection system gives
fire warnings and automatically stops the APU. The APU fire
warning light comes on to identify the correct fire switch to
use to extinguish the fire. The fire switch solenoid releases
the switch so that it can be pulled up. If the APU is running,
it stops when the fire switch is pulled. The fire switch isolates
the APU from the aircraft systems.

Fire Bottle Discharge
If the fire warnings do not go away with the switch out,
put the switch to the left or right DISCH position. Hold the

Engine Maintenance & Operation

Reciprocating Engine Overhaul
                                                                 applied to a part is essential in making decisions regarding
Both maintenance and complete engine overhauls are               visible wear. When the powerplant technician signs the release
performed normally at specified intervals. This interval is      for the return to service for an overhauled engine, this certifies
usually governed by the number of hours the powerplant           that the complete overhaul process has been performed using
has been in operation. The actual overhaul period for a          methods, techniques, and practices acceptable to the Federal
specific engine is generally determined by the manufacturer’s    Aviation Administration (FAA) Administrator.
recommendations. Each engine manufacturer sets a total
time in service when the engine should be removed from           Top Overhaul
service and overhauled. Depending upon how the engine            Reciprocating piston aircraft engines can be repaired by a
is used in service, the overhaul time can be mandatory. The      top overhaul. This means an overhaul of those parts on top of
overhaul time is listed in hours and is referred to as time      the crankcase, without completely dismantling the engine. It
before overhaul (TBO). For example, if an engine had a life      includes removal of the units (i.e., exhaust collectors, ignition
of 2,000 hours and had operated 500 hours, it would have a       harness, intake pipes) necessary to remove the cylinders. The
TBO of 1,500 hours. Tests and experience have shown that         actual top overhaul consists of reconditioning the engine’s
operation beyond this period of time could result in certain     cylinders by replacing or reconditioning the piston and
parts being worn beyond their safe limits. For an overhauled     piston rings, and reconditioning or plating the cylinder wall
engine to be as airworthy as a new one, worn parts, as well      and valve-operating mechanism, including valve guides if
as damaged parts, must be detected and replaced during           needed. A top overhaul is a little misleading, because it is
overhaul. The only way to detect all unairworthy parts is to     really an engine repair procedure and not a real overhaul
perform a thorough and complete overhaul process while the       as described earlier. Usually at this time, the accessories
engine is disassembled. The major purpose of overhaul is to      require no attention other than that normally required during
inspect, repair, and replace worn engine parts.                  ordinary maintenance functions. This repair is generally due
                                                                 to valves or piston rings wearing prematurely. Many stress
A complete overhaul process includes the following ten           that if an engine requires this much dismantling, it should
steps: receiving inspection; disassembly; visual inspection;     be completely disassembled and receive a major overhaul.
cleaning; structural inspection; non-destructive testing (NDT)
inspection; dimensional inspection; repair and replacement;      Major Overhaul & Major Repairs
reassembly; and testing and break in. The inspection phases      Major overhaul consists of the complete reconditioning of
are the most precise and the most important phases of the        the powerplant. A reciprocating engine would require that the
overhaul. Inspection cannot be slighted or performed in a        crankcase be disassembled per the FAA; a major overhaul
careless or incomplete manner. It is always recommended          is not generally a major repair. A certified powerplant-rated
that complete records be made of the inspection process and      technician can perform or supervise a major overhaul of an
kept with the engine records.                                    engine if it is not equipped with an internal supercharger
                                                                 or has a propeller reduction system other than spur-type
Each engine manufacturer provides very specific tolerances       gears. At regular intervals, an engine should be completely
to which the engine parts must conform and provides general      dismantled, thoroughly cleaned, and inspected. Each part
instructions to aid in determining the airworthiness of the      should be overhauled in accordance with the manufacturer’s
part. However, in many cases, the final determination must       instructions and tolerances for the engine involved. At this
be made by the technician. Although the determination must       time all accessories are removed, overhauled, and tested.
be made if the part is serviceable, repairable, or should be     Again, instructions from the manufacturer of the accessory
rejected, the technician should follow the manufacturer’s        concerned should be followed.
manuals and information. When dimensional tolerances
are concerned, the manufacturer publishes a new minimum          General Overhaul Procedures
and serviceable dimension for all critical component parts.

Knowledge of the operating principles, strength, and stresses
                                                                 types of engines in use, it is not possible to treat the specific

                                                                                                                             10-1

overhaul of each engine in this text. However, there are
various overhaul practices and instructions of a nonspecific
nature that apply to all makes and models of engines.

Any engine to be overhauled completely should receive a
runout check of its crankshaft or propeller shaft as a first
step. Any question concerning crankshaft or propeller shaft
replacement is resolved at this time, since a shaft whose
runout is beyond limits must be replaced.

Throughout the life of a product (whether type-certificated
or not), manufacturing defects, changes in service, or
design improvements often occur. When that happens, the
OEM frequently uses a safety bulletin (SB) to distribute
the information to the operator of the aircraft. SBs are
good information and should be strongly considered by the
owner for implementation to the aircraft. However, SBs are
not required unless they are referred to in an airworthiness
directive (AD) note or if compliance is required as a part of
the authorized inspection program. Refer to 14 CFR part 39,         Figure 10-1. Engine mounted on an overhaul stand.
section 39.27.
                                                                      3.   All loose studs, and loose or damaged fittings, should

                                                                           inspection.

numbers, and the accessories should be sent to overhaul
if needed. The overhaul records should be organized, and            Inspection Process
the appropriate manuals obtained and reviewed along with            The inspection of engine parts during overhaul is divided
a review of the engine’s history (log books). The engine’s          into three categories:
service bulletins, airworthiness directives, and type certificate

compliance should be checked. The exterior of the engine
should be cleaned after mounting it on an overhaul stand.             2.   Structural.
[Figure 10-1]                                                         3.   Dimensional.

Cleaning
After visually inspecting engine recesses for deposits of metal
particles, it is important to clean all engine parts thoroughly
to facilitate further inspection. Two processes for cleaning
engine parts are:
  1.   Degreasing to remove dirt and sludge (soft carbon).
  2.   The removal of hard carbon deposits by decarbonizing,
       brushing or scraping, and grit-blasting.

Degreasing
Degreasing can be done by immersing or spraying the part in a
suitable commercial solvent. [Figure 10-3] Extreme care must
be used if any water-mixed degreasing solutions containing        Figure 10-3. Typical solvent degreasing tank.


                                                                                                                              10-5

blasting parts for the abrasive material being used. Sand,         to 200 °F). Then the part should be washed thoroughly in
rice, baked wheat, plastic pellets, glass beads, or crushed        cold running water, dipped in hot water, and dried in an air
walnut shells are examples of abrasive substances that are         blast. Immediately thereafter, the part should be painted with
used for grit-blasting parts. A grit-blasting machine is shown     a prime coat and engine enamel in the same manner as that
in Figure 10-4.                                                    suggested for aluminum parts.

enamel on exterior surfaces. All remaining enamel should be
removed by grit-blasting, particularly in the crevices between     Structural Inspection
cylinder cooling fins.                                             One of the best methods to double check your visual
                                                                   inspection findings is to supplement them with one of the
At the conclusion of cleaning operations, rinse the part in        forms of nondestructive testing, such as magnetic particle,
petroleum solvent, dry and remove any loose particles of           dye penetrate, eddy current, ultrasound, and x-ray inspections.
carbon or other foreign matter by air-blasting, and apply a        Defects in nonmagnetic parts (aluminum parts) can be found

Magnesium parts should be cleaned thoroughly with a
dichromate treatment prior to painting. This treatment             Dye Penetrant Inspection
consists of cleaning all traces of grease and oil from the         Dye penetrant inspection is a nondestructive test for defects
part by using a neutral, noncorrosive degreasing medium            open to the surface in parts made of any nonporous material.
followed by a rinse, after which the part is immersed for at       It is used with equal success on such metals as aluminum,
least 45 minutes in a hot dichromate solution (three-fourths of    magnesium, brass, copper, cast iron, stainless steel, and
a pound of sodium dichromate to 1 gallon of water at 180 °F        titanium. Dye penetrant inspection uses a penetrating liquid
                                                                   that enters a surface opening and remains there, making it
                                                                   clearly visible to the inspector. It calls for visual examination
                                                                   of the part after it has been processed, increasing the visibility
                                                                   of the defect so that it can be detected. Visibility of the
                                                                   penetrating material is increased by the addition of one of two
                                                                   types of dye: visible or fluorescent. When using a fluorescent
                                                                   dye, the inspection is accomplished using an ultraviolet (UV)
                                                                   light source (black light).

                                                                   The steps for performing a dye penetrant inspection are:
                                                                     1.   Thorough cleaning of the metal surface.
                                                                     2.   Applying penetrant.
                                                                     3.   Removing penetrant with remover emulsifier or
                                                                          cleaner.

Magnetic Particle Inspection

Magnetic particle inspection is a method of detecting invisible
cracks and other defects in ferromagnetic materials, such as           1.   Maximum taper of cylinder walls.
iron and steel. It is not applicable to nonmagnetic materials.         2.   Maximum out-of-roundness.


                                                                                                                              10-7

                                                                    at the skirt of the cylinder to detect dents or bends caused by
                                                                    careless handling. A step, or ridge, is formed in the cylinder
                                                                    by the wearing action of the piston rings. [Figure 10-6] The
                                                                    greatest wear is at the top of the ring travel limit. The ridge
                                                                    that results is likely to cause damage to the rings or piston. If
                                                                    the step exceeds tolerances, it should be removed by grinding
                                                                    the cylinder oversize, or it should be blended by hand-stoning
                                                                    to break the sharp edge.

                                                                    A step also may be found where the bottom ring reaches the
                                                                    lowest travel. This step is rarely found to be excessive, but
                                                                    it should be checked.

                                                                    Inspect the cylinder walls for rust, pitting, or scores. Mild
                                                                    damage of this sort can be removed when the cylinders are

  4.   Step.                                                        repair stations, have an exchange service on cylinders with
  5.   Fit between piston skirt and cylinder.                       damaged barrels.

Taper of the cylinder walls is the difference between the           Rocker Arms & Shafts
diameter of the cylinder barrel at the bottom and the diameter      Inspect the valve rockers for cracks and worn, pitted, or scored
at the top. The cylinder is usually worn larger at the top than     tips. See that all oil passages are free from obstructions.
at the bottom. This taper is caused by the natural wear pattern.
At the top of the stroke, the piston is subjected to greater heat   Inspect the shaft’s diameter for correct size with a micrometer.
and pressure and more erosive environment than at the bottom        Rocker shafts are often found to be scored and burned because
of the stroke. Also, there is greater freedom of movement           of excessive turning in the cylinder head. Also, there may be
at the top of the stroke. Under these conditions, the piston        some pickup on the shaft (bronze from the rocker bushing
wears the cylinder wall more at the top of the cylinder. In         transferred to the steel shaft). Generally, this is caused
most cases, the taper ends with a ridge, that must be removed       by overheating and too little clearance between shaft and
during overhaul. [Figure 10-6]                                      bushing. The clearance between the shaft and the bushing
                                                                    is most important.

Figure 10-6. Ridge or step formed in an engine cylinder.


Crankshaft
Use extreme care in inspecting and checking the crankshaft
for straightness. Place the crankshaft in V-blocks, supported
at the locations specified in the applicable engine overhaul
manual as in Figure 10-7. Using a surface plate and a dial
indicator, measure the shaft runout. If the total indicator
reading exceeds the dimensions given in the manufacturer’s
table of limits, the shaft must not be re-used. A bent crankshaft
should not be straightened. Any attempt to do so results
in rupture of the nitrided surface of the bearing journals,
a condition that causes eventual failure of the crankshaft.
Measure the outside diameter of the crankshaft main and rod-
bearing journals using a micrometer. [Figure 10-8] Internal
measurements can be made by using telescoping gauges, and
then measuring the telescoping gauge with a micrometer.
[Figure 10-9] Compare the resulting measurements with               Figure 10-7. Checking crankshaft runout.
those in the table of limits.

Checking Alignment
Check bushings that have been replaced to determine if the
bushing and rod bores are square and parallel to each other.
The alignment of a connecting rod can be checked several
ways. One method requires a push fit arbor for each end of
the connecting rod, a surface plate, and two parallel blocks
of equal height.

To measure rod squareness, or twist, insert the arbors into
the rod bores. [Figure 10-10] Place the parallel blocks on          Figure 10-8. A micrometer.


                                                                                                                    10-9

                                                                 be impractical to list all the steps involved in the overhaul
                                                                 of an engine. It should be understood that there are other
                                                                 operations and inspections that must be performed. For exact
                                                                 information regarding a specific engine model, consult the
                                                                 manufacturer’s overhaul manual.

                                                                 Repair & Replacement
                                                                 The engine components that have failed inspection, or are
                                                                 unrepairable, should have been discarded. The component
                                                                 parts that need repair and replacement are now given the
                                                                 attention required. The replacement components (new parts)
                                                                 are organized and laid out for reassembly.

                                                                 Minor damage to engine parts, such as burrs, nicks, scratches,
                                                                 scoring, or galling, should be removed with a fine oil stone,
Figure 10-9. Telescoping gauges and micrometer combination.      crocus cloth, or any similar abrasive substance. Following
                                                                 any repairs of this type, the part should be cleaned carefully
                                                                 to be certain that all abrasive has been removed, and then
                                                                 checked with its mating part to assure that the clearances
                                                                 are not excessive. Flanged surfaces that are bent, warped,
                                                                 or nicked can be repaired by lapping to a true surface on a
                                                                 surface plate. Again, the part should be cleaned to be certain
                                                                 that all abrasive has been removed. Defective threads can
                                                                 sometimes be repaired with a suitable die or tap. Small
                                                                 nicks can be satisfactorily removed with Swiss pattern files
                                                                 or small, edged stones. Pipe threads should not be tapped
                                                                 deeper to clean them, because this practice results in an
                                                                 oversized tapped hole. If galling or scratches are removed
                                                                 from a bearing surface of a journal, it should be buffed to a
                                                                 high polished finish.

Cylinder and piston assemblies are inspected according              not be re-used. The edge thickness can be measured with

Dents or bends in the fins should be left alone unless there        inspect it using a structural inspection method described later.
is danger of cracking. Where pieces of fin are missing, the         Examine the valve springs for cracks, rust, broken ends, and
sharp edges should be filed to a smooth contour. Fin breakage       compression. Cracks can be located by visual inspection or
in a concentrated area causes dangerous local hot spots. Fin        the magnetic particle method.
breakage near the spark plug bushings or on the exhaust side
of the cylinder is obviously more dangerous than in other
areas. When removing or re-profiling a cylinder fin, follow
the instructions and the limits in the manufacturer’s manual.

Inspect spark plug inserts for the condition of the threads
and for looseness. Run a tap of the proper size through the
bushing. Very often, the inside threads of the bushing are
burned. If more than one thread is missing, the bushing
should be rejected. Tighten a plug in the bushing to check
for looseness.

Piston & Piston Pins
If the old piston is to be reused, or a new piston is to be used,
measure the outside of the piston by means of a micrometer.
Measurements must be taken in several directions and on
the skirt, as well as on the lands section. Check these sizes
against the cylinder size. Most engines use cam ground
pistons to compensate for the greater expansion parallel to
the pin during engine operation. The diameter of these pistons
measures several thousandths of an inch larger at an angle to
the piston pin hole, than parallel to the pin hole. Inspect the
ring grooves for evidence of wear. The groove needs to be           Figure 10-11. Checking a piston pin for bends.


                                                                                                                            10-11

     Face of valve must run true with stem within 0.0015-inch


                                     Valve face. Do not include
           A                         when measuring thickness


                                                      A


Figure 10-12. Valve showing locations for checking runout and
section for measuring edge thickness.                             Figure 10-14. Checking valve stretch with a manufacturer’s gauge.


Critical areas of the valve include the face and tip
[Figure 10-13], both of which should be examined for
pitting and excessive wear. Minor pitting on valve faces can
sometimes be removed by grinding.

Inspect the valve for stretch and wear using a micrometer
or a valve radius gauge. [Figure 10-14] If a micrometer is
used, stretch is found as a smaller diameter of the valve stem
near the neck of the valve. Measure the diameter of the valve
stem and check the fit of the valve in its guide.

Examine the valve visually for physical damage and damage
from burning or corrosion. Do not re-use valves that indicate
damage of this nature.

Compression is tested with a valve spring compression tester.
[Figure 10-15] The spring is compressed until its total height
is that specified by the manufacturer. The dial on the tester
should indicate the pressure, in pounds, required to compress


       Valve face

pressure limits established by the manufacturer.                      cylinder, and an expander screw is inserted in the pilot from
                                                                      the top of the guide. [Figure 10-17] The pilot must be tight in

The valve seat inserts of aircraft engine cylinders usually           fluid hose is inserted through one of the spark plug inserts.

of aluminum bronze or phosphor bronze alloys. Steel seats

The interference fit is used to obtain a more positive seal by
means of a narrow contact surface. [Figure 10-23] Theoretically,
there is a line contact between the valve and seat. With this
line contact, the load that the valve exerts against the seat
is concentrated in a very small area, thereby increasing the               Figure 10-23. Interference fit of valve and valve seat.
unit load at any one spot. The interference fit is especially
beneficial during the first few hours of operation after an                Install the valve into the chuck and adjust the chuck so that
overhaul. The positive seal reduces the possibility of a burned            the valve face is approximately 2 inches from the chuck.
valve or seat that a leaking valve might produce. After the                [Figure 10-24] If the valve is chucked any further out,
first few hours of running, these angles tend to pound down                there is danger of excessive wobble and also a possibility of
and become identical.                                                      grinding into the stem.

approves it.                                                               refacing a valve.

The valve is held by a clamp on the side of the stone.
[Figure 10-27] With the machine and grinding fluid turned on,
the valve is pushed lightly against the stone and swung back
and forth. Do not swing the valve stem off either edge of the
stone. Because of the tendency for the valve to overheat during        Smooth, even                                 Sharply
this grinding, be sure plenty of grinding fluid covers the tip.         gray band                                defined edges

Grinding of the valve tip may remove, or partially remove,
the bevel on the edge of the valve. To restore this bevel,
mount a V-way approximately 45° to the grinding stone.
Hold the valve onto the V-way and twist the valve tip onto
the stone. With a light touch, grind all the way around the
tip. This bevel prevents scratching the valve guide when the
valve is installed.                                                Figure 10-28. A correctly lapped valve.

contact area. The appearance of a correctly lapped valve is
shown in Figure 10-28.                                             The most common repair is the removal of scores. Usually,

indications are of value in diagnosing improper valve and          be done to increase the piston weight.
valve seat grinding. Incorrect indications, their cause, and

                       Stone
                                                                   reusing old ones, especially during overhaul.

                                                                   Cylinder Grinding & Honing
                                                                   If a cylinder has excessive taper, out-of-roundness, step, or
                                                                   its maximum size is beyond limits, it can be reground to
                                                                   the next allowable oversize. If the cylinder walls are lightly
                                                                   rusted, scored, or pitted, the damage may be removed by
                                                                   honing or lapping.

                                                                   Regrinding a cylinder is a specialized job that the powerplant
Figure 10-27. Grinding a valve tip.
                                                                   mechanic is not usually expected to be able to do. However,

the mechanic must be able to recognize when a cylinder             The standard used when measuring the finish of a cylinder
needs regrinding, and they must know what constitutes a            wall is known as micro-inch root mean square (micro-inch
good or bad job.                                                   RMS). In a finish where the depth of the grinding scratches
                                                                   are one-millionth (0.000001) of an inch deep, it is specified
Generally, standard aircraft cylinder oversizes are 0.010 inch,    as 1 micro-inch RMS. Most aircraft cylinders are ground to
0.015 inches, 0.020 inch, or 0.030 inch. Aircraft cylinders        a finish of 15 to 20 micro-inch RMS. Several low-powered
have relatively thin walls and may have a nitrided surface,        engines have cylinders that are ground to a relatively
that must not be ground away. Nitriding is a surface hardening     rough 20- to 30-micro-inch RMS finish. On the other end
process that hardens the steel surface to a depth of several       of the scale, some manufacturers require a superfinish of
thousandths of an inch. Any one manufacturer usually does          approximately 4- to 6-micro-inch RMS.
not allow all of the above oversizes. Some manufacturers do
not allow regrinding to an oversize at all. The manufacturer’s     Cylinder grinding is accomplished by a firmly mounted stone
overhaul manual, or parts catalog, usually lists the oversizes     that revolves around the cylinder bore, as well as up and down
allowed for a particular make and model engine.                    the length of the cylinder barrel. [Figure 10-30] The cylinder,
                                                                   the stone, or both may move to get this relative movement.
To determine the regrind size, the standard bore size              The size of the grind is determined by the distance the stone
must be known. This usually can be determined from the             is set away from the centerline of the cylinder. Some cylinder
manufacturer’s specifications or manuals. The regrind size is      bore grinding machines produce a perfectly straight bore,
figured from the standard bore. For example, a certain cylinder    while others are designed to grind a choked bore. A choked
has a standard bore of 3.875 inches. To have a cylinder ground     bore grind refers to the manufacturing process in which
to 0.015 inches oversize, it is necessary to grind to a bore       the cylinder walls arc ground to produce a smaller internal
diameter of 3.890 inch (3.875 + 0.015). A tolerance of ±0.0005     diameter at the top than at the bottom. The purpose of this
inches is usually accepted for cylinder grinding.                  type grind or taper is to maintain a straight cylinder wall
                                                                   during operation. As a cylinder heats up during operation,
Another factor to consider when determining the size to which      the head and top of the cylinder are subjected to more heat
a cylinder must be reground is the maximum wear that has           than the bottom. This causes greater expansion at the top than
occurred. If there are spots in the cylinder wall that are worn    at the bottom, thereby maintaining the desired straight wall.
larger than the first oversize, then obviously it is necessary
to grind to the next oversize to clean up the entire cylinder.     After grinding a cylinder, it may be necessary to hone the
                                                                   cylinder bore to produce the desired finish. In this case,
The type of finish desired in the cylinder is an important         specify the cylinder regrind size to allow for some metal
consideration when ordering a regrind. Some engine                 removal during honing. The usual allowance for honing is
manufacturers specify a fairly rough finish on the cylinder        0.001 inch. If a final cylinder bore size of 3.890 inches is
walls, that allows the rings to seat even if they are not lapped   desired, specify the regrind size of 3.889 inches, and then
to the cylinder. Other manufacturers desire a smooth finish        hone to 3.890 inches.
to which a lapped ring seats without much change in ring

expensive to produce.                                              hones. The burnishing hone is used only to produce the

micromatic hone can also be used to straighten out the                                          Stone
cylinder walls. A burnishing hone should not be used in an
attempt to straighten cylinder walls. [Figure 10-31] Since
the stones are only spring loaded, they follow the contour
of the cylinder wall and may aggravate a tapered condition.

Deglazing the cylinder walls is accomplished with the use
of a deglazing hone. A cross-hatch pattern must be placed
on the cylinder wall to allow for piston ring break-in. This
is accomplished by a deglazing hone turned by a drill being
moved in and out of the cylinder rapidly. [Figure 10-32]                             Cylinder


After the cylinders have been reground or deglazed, or both,        Figure 10-31. Cylinder honing.
check the size and wall finish, and check for evidence of
overheating or grinding cracks before installing on an engine.

Reassembly
Before starting reassembly, all serviceable and new engine
components need to be cleaned, organized, and laid out in the
order they are to be assembled. A popular method of engine
assembly is for the engine to be assembled at one work station
with the same technicians completing the total assembly of
the engine. It is also important to refer to the parts catalog to
ensure that the correct hardware is used during the assembly
of the engine. The engine overhaul manual should be referred        Figure 10-32. Cross-hatch pattern on cylinder wall.
to for information on the use of safety wire, self-locking
nuts, and torque values. During assembly, the components            Installation & Testing
should be pre-lubricated as the overhaul manual sets forth. It      Testing Reciprocating Engines
is important to follow the manufacturer’s overhaul assembly         The procedures and equipment used in determining that
procedures completely and perform all checks and procedures         an engine is ready for airworthy service and is in excellent
that are called for in the manual.                                  mechanical condition, normally requires the use of a
                                                                    test stand, or test cell, although the aircraft can be used.

                                                                                                                          10-19

[Figure 10-33] The method of engine testing or run-in that          121 °C. Near the end of final run-in, corrosion-preventive
takes place during overhaul prior to delivery of the engine         mixture (CPM) is used as the engine lubricant. The engine
is critical to the airworthiness of the engine. It must be          induction passages and combustion chambers are also
emphasized that engine run-in is as vital as any other phase        treated with CPM by an aspiration method. CPM is drawn
of engine overhaul, for it is the means by which the quality        or breathed into the engine.
of a new or newly overhauled engine is checked, and it is
the final step in the preparation of an engine for service.         Test Cell Requirements

Measured at the carburetor entrance, carburetor air temperature

many other important items of information.                                     150                         150           50

its components, or the fluids flowing through it, affects

charge density, but also the vaporization of the fuel. CAT
is also useful for checking induction system condition.                                        C°                        Yellow
