Troubleshooting                                                    After each flight, activate choke for a moment before stopping
Follow an organized method of troubleshooting. This                engine. Close all engine openings like exhaust pipe, venting
facilitates the identification of discrepancies or malfunctions.   tube, and air filter to prevent entry of contamination and
                                                                   humidity. For engine storage of one to four weeks, proceed
  •    Fuel—start by checking the supply (tank), fittings

       (loose), filter (plugged), and float chamber (fouled).
                                                                   operating temperature. Let the engine run at increased idle
  •    Spark—check for spark at the spark plugs.                   speed. Shut the engine down and secure against inadvertent
                                                                   engine start. Remove air filters and inject approximately
Problems of a more complex nature are best left to an engine       3 cubic cm of preservation oil or equivalent oil into the
technician. The following are examples of engine troubles          air intake of each carburetor. Restart the engine and run at
and potential fixes.                                               increased idle speed for 10–15 seconds. Shut engine down
                                                                   and secure against inadvertent engine start. Close all engine
Engine Keeps Running With Ignition OFF                             openings, such as exhaust pipe, venting tube, and air filter,
Possible cause: Overheating of engine.                             to prevent entry of contamination and humidity.
Remedy: Let engine cool down at idling at approximately
2,000 engine rpm.                                                  For engine storage of engine for longer than four weeks and
                                                                   up to one year, proceed with preservation prior to engine stop
Knocking Under Load                                                and on the engine at operating temperature. Let the engine
Possible cause: Octane rating of fuel too low.                     run at increased idle speed. Remove air filters and inject
Remedy: Use fuel with higher octane rating.                        approximately 6 cubic cm of preservative oil or equivalent
Possible cause: Fuel starvation, lean mixture.                     oil into the air intake of each carburetor. Stop the engine.
Remedy: Check fuel supply.                                         Remove spark plugs and inject approximately 6 cubic cm
                                                                   preservation oil or equivalent oil into each cylinder and

Two methods can be used to check the cylinders compression.         age the oil lines and lead to hoses slipping off the cooler.
The compression gauge method is used to measure
compression using a compression tracer. Readings are taken          Carburetor Adjustment & Checks
with a fully open throttle valve at engine oil temperature          To adjust the engine’s idle speed, adjust the idle stop screw
between 30 °C and 70 °C (90 °F to 160 °F). If readings are          (7 mm screw) against throttle lever. Standard idle mixture
below 6 bar (90 psi) a check of the pistons, cylinders, valves,     screw position is 1¼ turns out from the seated position. Fine
and cylinder heads must be undertaken.                              adjustment may be necessary to give a smooth idle.

loss of 25 percent. As an alternative to a compression test, a      the mixture may be affected by operation with a propeller

sealing, and valves. This is the normal test used in aviation       to be used in these situations, an exhaust gas temperature
and requires specific equipment. The test is carried out with       (EGT) gauge should be fitted and monitored against the
the engine in warm to hot condition. Input pressure is best         limits specified above. Do not change carburetor settings if

Note: The propeller needs to be restrained. A differential          for altitude. Visual inspection should include checks for
of lower than 80/60 (generally a 25 percent loss) indicates         carburetor joint degradation and carburetor linkage for full
a problem.                                                          and free movement, correct positioning of stops and security.

Problems can be better identified by observing where air is         Spark Plugs
escaping from the cylinder, blow-by. Some examples are as           When plugs are removed from a warm engine, the inspection
follows:                                                            of the tip of the spark plug can be used to indicate the health
  1.   Blow-by through the crankcase vent indicates worn            of the engine. If the tip of the plug is a light brown color,
       rings or bore.                                               the plug is operating correctly. A black velvet, sooty looking
                                                                    plug tip generally is an indication of an overly rich mixture
  2.   Leaking from carburetor indicates a poor intake valve        (check the choke, air filter, and intake). If the firing end tip
       seal.                                                        is covered with oil, it is an indication of too much oil in
  3.   Leaking from exhaust indicates a poor exhaust valve          the combustion chamber (check for worn piston rings and
       seal.                                                        cylinder walls). When servicing the spark plugs, do not
  4.   Head leak indicates poor head to cylinder seal.              use steel or brass brushes for cleaning, and never sandblast
                                                                    plugs. Clean the spark plugs with a plastic brush in a solvent.
With the problem identified, the malfunction can then               Check electrode gap and, if necessary, adjust to 0.55–0.6mm
be corrected. Poor compression can be an indication of a            (0.022 in–0.024 in) by carefully bending the electrode. Use
serious problem. For example, continued operation with              the recommended Plugs (NGK D9EA) and place a suitable

to eventual valve failure and heavy damage to the piston,           them in the engine. Tighten spark plugs when the engine is
connecting-rod, barrel, and head.                                   cold and adjust engine to the correct torque value. Reconnect

Lubrication System
                                                                    Exhaust System
The oil should be changed as required by the manufacturer.
When changed, the oil filter should also be changed. Change         Visually check the exhaust system for security of mounting,
the oil filter at every 50 hourly inspection. Drain the oil while   damage, rubbing, leaks, and general condition. Check nuts
engine is still warm and visually check for leaks. Fill the         and bolts for tightness and condition; re-torque and replace
engine with oil (approximately 2.3 liters) and check oil level.     if necessary.
Never exceed the maximum mark. Use only registered brand
                                                                    Head Bolts
oils meeting the correct specifications. Do not drain the oil
cooler during a normal oil change. The cooler holds only a          Check the head bolt torque after five hours of operation, and
small amount of old oil that has negligible effect on the new       again after ten hours of operation. The bolts should, thereafter,
oil. Taking the hoses on and off the cooler can prematurely         be checked annually. Head bolts torque when cold to 20 ft/lb.


                                                                                                                             11-24

Tachometer & Sender
Many apparent engine problems can be caused through
inaccurate tachometers. Where engine performance is
observed to be outside limits, the tachometer should be
checked against a calibrated instrument. Tachometer sender
gap is 0.4mm (0.016 inches). The sender must have at least
60 percent covered by the tags fitted to the gearbox side of
the flywheel. Ensure both tags are equal distance from sender.

using these charts. [Figure 11-35]

A                                                                   compartment, the partition moves over, further compressing
                                                                    the air which holds pressure on the oil.
Abradable strip. A strip of material in the compressor
housing of some axial-flow gas turbine engines. The tip of          AD (ashless dispersant) oil. A mineral-based lubricating oil
the compressor blade touches the abradable strip, and wears,        used in reciprocating engines. This oil does not contain any
or abrades a groove in it. This groove ensures the minimum          metallic ash-forming additives, but has additives that disperse
tip clearance.                                                      the contaminants and hold them in suspension until they can
                                                                    be removed by filters.
Abradable tip (compressor blade tip). The tip of some
axial-flow compressor blades constructed so that it will            ADC. Air data computer. An electronic computer in an
abrade, or wear away, upon contact with the compressor              aircraft that senses pitot pressure, static pressure, and total air
housing, which ensures the minimum tip clearance between            temperature. It produces an indication of altitude, indicated
the blade and the housing.                                          airspeed, true airspeed, and Mach number. The output of ADC
                                                                    is usable by any of the engine or flight control computers.
Absolute pressure. Pressure referenced from zero pressure
or a vacuum.                                                        ADI (antidetonation injection) system. A system used with
                                                                    some large reciprocating engines in which a mixture of water
AC. Alternating current. Electrical current in which                and alcohol is sprayed into the engine with the fuel when
the electrons continually change their rate of flow and             operating at extremely high power. The air/fuel mixture is
periodically reverse their direction.                               leaned to allow the engine to develop its maximum power,
                                                                    and the ADI fluid absorbs excessive heat when it vaporizes.
ACC. Active clearance control. A system for controlling the
clearance between tips of the compressor and turbine blades         Adiabatic change. A physical change that takes place within
and the case of high-performance turbofan engines. When             a material in which heat energy is neither added to the
the engine is operating at maximum power, the blade tip             material, nor taken away. If a container of gas is compressed,
clearance should be minimum, and the ACC system sprays              with no heat energy added to or taken from it, the gas will
cool fan discharge air over the outside of the engine case. This    become hotter; its temperature will rise.
causes the case to shrink enough to decrease the tip clearance.
For flight conditions that do not require such close clearance,     Aeromatic propeller. A patented variable-pitch propeller
the cooling air is turned off, and the case expands to its normal   that has flyweights around the blade shanks and the
dimensions. The control of the ACC system is done by the            blades angled back from the hub to increase the effects of
FADEC, or full-authority digital electronic control.                aerodynamic and centrifugal twisting forces. This propeller
                                                                    automatically maintains a relatively constant rpm for any
Acceleration. The amount the velocity of an object is               throttle setting.
increased by a force during each second it is acted upon by
that force. Acceleration is usually measured and expressed          Aft-fan engine. A turbofan engine with the fan mounted
in terms of feet per second, per second (fps²).                     behind the compressor section. The blades of an aft-fan are
                                                                    normally extensions of the free turbine blades.
Accessory end. The end of a reciprocating engine on which
many of the accessories are mounted. Also, called the anti-         Afterburner. A component in the exhaust system of a
propeller end.                                                      turbojet or turbofan engine used to increase the thrust for
                                                                    takeoff and for special flight conditions. Since much of the air
Accumulator. A hydraulic component that stores a                    passing through a gas turbine engine is used only for cooling,
non-compressible fluid, such as oil, under pressure. An             it still contains a great deal of oxygen. Fuel is sprayed into
accumulator has two compartments separated by a flexible            the hot, oxygen-rich exhaust in the afterburner, where it is
or movable partition with one compartment containing                ignited and burned to produce additional thrust.
compressed air. When oil is pumped into the other


                                                                                                                                  G-1

Air bleed (carburetor component). A small hole in the fuel
passage between the float bowl and the discharge nozzle of          Analog indicator. An indicator that shows the value of
a float carburetor. Air drawn into the liquid fuel through the      the parameter being measured by a number marked on a
air bleed breaks the fuel up into an emulsion, making it easy       graduated dial aligned with a movable pointer.
to atomize and vaporize.
                                                                    Angle of attack. The acute angle between the chordline of
Air cooling. The removal of unwanted heat from an aircraft          a propeller blade and the relative wind. The angle of attack
engine by transferring the heat directly into the air flowing       is affected by both the engine rpm and the forward speed of
over the engine components.                                         the aircraft.

Air/fuel mixture ratio. The ratio of the weight of the air          Annual inspection. A complete inspection of the airframe and
to that of the fuel in the mixture fed into the cylinders of        powerplant required for FAA-certificated aircraft operating
an engine.                                                          under 14 CFR part 91 General Operating and Flight Rules,
                                                                    and not on one of the authorized special inspection programs.
Air impingement starter. A turbine engine starter that              An annual inspection must be conducted every 12 calendar
basically consists of a nozzle that blows a stream of               months, and it must be conducted by an aviation maintenance
compressed air against the turbine blades to rotate the             technician who holds an Airframe and Powerplant rating
compressor for starting the engine.                                 and an Inspection Authorization. The scope of an annual
                                                                    inspection is the same as that of a 100-hour inspection.
Air-oil separator. A component in a turbine engine
lubrication system that removes the air from the scavenged          Annular duct. A duct, or passage, that surrounds an object.
oil before it is returned to the oil tank.                          The annular fan-discharge duct surrounds the core engine.

Airworthiness Directive. Airworthiness Directives (ADs)             Annular orifice. A ring-shaped orifice, normally one that
are legally enforceable regulations issued by the FAA               surrounds another orifice.
in accordance with 14 CFR part 39 to correct an unsafe
condition in a product. Part 39 defines a product as an aircraft,   Annulus. A ring or groove around the outside of a circular
engine, propeller, or appliance.                                    body or shaft, or around the inside of a cylindrical hole.

All-weather spark plug. A shielded spark plug designed for          Annunciator panel. A panel of warning lights visible to the
high altitude operation. The ceramic insulator is recessed into     flight crew. The lights are identified by the name of the system
the shell to allow a resilient grommet on the ignition harness      they represent and are often covered with colored lenses. Red
to provide a watertight seal. All weather spark plugs, also         lights indicate a dangerous condition and green indicate a
called high-altitude spark plugs, are identified by their 3/4-      safe condition.
20 shielding threads.
                                                                    Anodizing. A hard, airtight, unbroken oxide film
Alpha control range (alpha mode). The flight operating              electrolytically deposited on an aluminum alloy surface to
mode from takeoff through landing for a turbo-prop engine.          protect it from corrosion.
Alpha mode includes operations from 95% to 100% of the
engine’s rated rpm.                                                 Anti-icing. Prevention of the formation of ice on a surface.

Altitude engine. An aircraft reciprocating engine equipped          Anti-propeller end. The end of a reciprocating engine that
with a supercharger that allows it to maintain its rated sea-       does not attach to the propeller. Also called the accessory end.
level horsepower to an established higher altitude.
                                                                    APC. Absolute pressure controller.
Amateur-built aircraft. Aircraft built by individuals as
a hobby rather than by factories as commercial products.            APU. Auxiliary power unit. A small turbine- or reciprocating-
Amateur-built or home-built aircraft do not fall under the          engine-powered generator, hydraulic pump, and air pump.
stringent requirements imposed by the FAA on commercially           APUs are installed in the aircraft and are used to supply
built aircraft.                                                     electrical power, air, and hydraulic pressure when the main
                                                                    engines are not running.
Ambient air pressure. The pressure of the air that surrounds
an object.                                                          Aramid fiber. Fiber made from an organic compound of


                                                                                                                               G-2

carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, and nitrogen. It has high strength       shaft on which the turbine wheel is mounted.
and low density. It is flexible under load and is able to
withstand impact, shock, and vibration. Kevlar is a well-          Axial-flow compressor. A type of compressor used in
known aramid fiber.                                                gas turbine engines. Air passes through the compressor
                                                                   in essentially a straight line, parallel to the axis of the
Aromatic compound. A chemical compound such as                     compressor. The compressor is made of a number of stages
toluene, xylene, and benzene that is blended with gasoline         of rotating compressor blades between stages of stationary
to improve its anti-detonation characteristics.                    stator vanes.

Articulating rod. See link rod.                                    Axis of rotation. The center line about which a propeller
                                                                   rotates.
Aspect ratio. The ratio of the length of an airfoil, such as a
compressor blade, to its width.                                    B
Asymmetrical loading. The loading of a propeller disc that         Babbitt. A soft silvery metal used for main bearing inserts
causes one side to produce more thrust than the other side.        in aircraft reciprocating engines. Babbitt is made of tin with
                                                                   small amounts of copper and antimony.
ATF. Aerodynamic twisting force. The aerodynamic force
that acts on a rotating propeller blade to increase its blade      Back (propeller nomenclature). The curved surface of a
angle. The axis of rotation of a blade is near the center of its   propeller blade. The back of a propeller blade corresponds
chordline, and the center of pressure is between the axis and      to the upper surface of an airplane wing.
the leading edge. Aerodynamic lift acting through the center
of pressure tries to rotate the blade to a higher pitch angle.     Back-suction mixture control. A type of mixture control
                                                                   used in some float carburetors that regulates the air/fuel
Atomize. The process of breaking a liquid down into tiny           mixture by varying the air pressure above the fuel in the
droplets or a fine spray. Atomized liquids vaporize easily.        float bowl.

Augmentor tube. A long, specially shaped stainless steel           Baffle. A thin sheet metal shroud or bulkhead used to direct
tube mounted around the exhaust tail pipe of a reciprocating       the flow of cooling air between and around the cylinder fins
engine. As exhaust gases flow through the augmentor tube,          of an air-cooled reciprocating engine.
they produce a low pressure in the engine compartment that
draws in cooling air through the cylinder fins.                    Bayonet stack. An exhaust stack with an elongated and
                                                                   flattened end. The gases leave the stack through a slot
Automatic intake valve. An intake valve opened by low              perpendicular to its length. Bayonet stacks decrease both
pressure created inside the cylinder as the piston moves down.     exhaust back pressure and noise.
There is no mechanical means of opening it.
                                                                   BDC. Bottom dead center. The position of a piston in a
Automatic mixture control (AMC). The device in a fuel              reciprocating engine when the piston is at the bottom of
metering system, such as a carburetor or fuel injection system,    its stroke, and the wrist pin, crankpin, and center of the
that keeps the air/fuel mixture ratio constant as the density      crankshaft are all in line.
of air changes with altitude.
                                                                   Bell mouth. The shape of the inlet of an augmentor tube
Autosyn system. The registered trade name of a remote              that forms a smooth converging duct. The bell mouth shape
indicating instrument system. An Autosyn system uses an            allows the maximum amount of air to be drawn into the tube.
electromagnet rotor, excited with 400-hertz AC, and a three-
phase distributed-pole stator.                                     Bell mouth inlet duct. A form of convergent inlet-air duct
                                                                   used to direct air into the compressor of a gas turbine engine.
Axial bearing load. The load on a bearing parallel to the          It is extremely efficient, and is used where there is little ram
shaft on which the bearing is mounted. Thrust produces an          pressure available to force air into the engine. Bell mouth
axial load on a bearing.                                           ducts are used in engine test cells and on engines installed
                                                                   in helicopters.
Axial turbine. A turbine that is turned by a fluid flowing
through it in a direction that is approximately parallel to the    Benzene. A colorless, volatile, flammable, aromatic


                                                                                                                              G-3

hydrocarbon liquid which has the chemical formula C6H6.             Blending. A method of repairing damaged compressor
Benzene, which is sometimes called benzoil, is used as a            and turbine blades. The damage is removed and the area is
solvent, a cleaning fluid, and a fuel for some special types        cleaned out with a fine file to form a shallow depression with
of reciprocating engines.                                           generous radii. The file marks are then removed with a fine
                                                                    abrasive stone so the surface of the repaired area will match
Bernoulli’s principle. A physical principle that explains           the surface of the rest of the blade.
the relationship between kinetic and potential energy in a
stream of moving fluid. When energy is neither added to             Blisk. A turbine wheel machined from a single slab of steel.
nor taken from the fluid, any increase in its velocity (kinetic     The disc and blades are an integral unit.
energy) will result in a corresponding decrease in its pressure
(potential energy).                                                 Blow-in doors. Spring-loaded doors in the inlet duct of some
                                                                    turbojet or turbofan engine installations that are opened by
Beta control range (Beta mode). The range of operation              differential air pressure when inlet air pressure drops below
of a turboprop powerplant used for in-flight approach and           that of the ambient air. Air flowing through the doors adds
ground handling of the engine and aircraft. Typically, the          to the normal inlet air passing through the engine and helps

allowed to sit.                                                     prevents the hot gases from circulating over the rear face of

filter element.                                                     no change in the total amount of energy as air passes through

commonly called a planetary gear train.                             which the fuel releases its energy outside of the engine.

                                                                    used on many smaller gas-turbine engines. It is used to start
Springback. A condition in the rigging of an aircraft engine        the engine, and when the engine is running, its circuitry is
control in which the stop at the engine is reached before the       shifted so that it acts as a generator.
stop in the flight deck. The flight deck control moves slightly
after the stop in the engine is reached, and when it is released,   Static pressure. The pressure of an unmoving fluid.
it springs back slightly.
                                                                    Static rpm. The number of revolutions per minute an aircraft
Spur-gear pump. A form of constant-displacement fluid               engine can produce when the aircraft is not moving.
pump that uses two meshing spur-gears mounted in a close
fitting housing. Fluid is taken into the housing where it fills     Steam cooling. A method of liquid cooling in which the
the space between the teeth of the gears and is carried around      coolant, normally water, is allowed to absorb enough heat
the housing as the gears rotate. On the discharge side of the       that it boils. The steam gives up its heat when it condenses
pump, the teeth of the two gears mesh, and the fluid is forced      back into a liquid.
out of the pump.
                                                                    Stellite. A nonferrous alloy of cobalt, chromium, and tungsten.
Squat switch. An electrical switch actuated by the landing          Stellite is hard, water resistant, and corrosion resistant, and it
gear scissors on the oleo strut. When no weight is on the           does not soften until its temperature is extremely high. Stellite
landing gear, the oleo piston is extended and the switch is in      is welded to the faces of many reciprocating engine exhaust
one position; but when weight is on the gear, the oleo strut        valves that operate at very high temperatures.
compresses and the switch changes its position.

Squealer tip (compressor blade tip). See profile tip.               shaft position is changed in steps by pulses from the control
                                                                    device. Stepping motors can make high-torque changes in
Squeeze film bearings. Another name for oil-damped                  small angular increments to their output shaft.
bearings. See oil-damped bearings.
                                                                    Stoichiometric mixture. The air/fuel mixture ratio that,
Stage length. The distance between landing points in airline        when burned, leaves no uncombined oxygen nor any free
operation.                                                          carbon. It releases the maximum amount of heat, and
                                                                    therefore produces the highest exhaust gas temperature. A
Stage of a compressor. One disc of rotor blades and the             stoichiometric mixture of gasoline and air contains 15 pounds
following set of stator vanes in an axial-flow compressor.          of air for 1 pound of gasoline.

Staggered timing. Ignition timing that causes the spark plug        Straight-through combustor. A combustor in a gas turbine
nearest the exhaust valve to fire a few degrees of crankshaft       engine through which the air from the compressor to the
rotation before the spark plug nearest the intake valve.            turbine flows in an essentially straight line.

Standard day conditions. Conditions that have been decided          Stratosphere. The upper part of the Earth’s atmosphere. The
upon by the ICAO for comparing all aircraft and engine              stratosphere extends upward from the tropopause, which is
performance. The most basic standard day conditions are:            approximately 36,000 feet above the surface of the Earth, to
temperature, 15 ºC or 59 ºF; altitude, mean sea level; pressure,    approximately 85,000 feet. The temperature of the air in the
29.92 inches of mercury.                                            stratosphere remains constant at -56.5 ºC (-69.7 ºF).

Standard J-1. A World War I training airplane powered by            Stress. A force within an object that tries to prevent an outside
a Curtiss OX-5 engine.                                              force from changing its shape.

Standpipe. A pipe which protrudes upward from the base              Stroboscopic tachometer. A tachometer used to measure
of an oil tank and through which oil used for normal engine         the speed of any rotating device without physical contact.
lubrication is drawn. In the event of a catastrophic leak when      A highly accurate variable-frequency oscillator triggers a
all oil available to the engine-driven pump is lost overboard,      high-intensity strobe light. When the lamp is flashing at the
enough oil is available from an outlet below the standpipe to       same frequency the device is rotating, the device appears to

stand still.                                                       of time an engine should be operated between overhauls
                                                                   without normal wear causing parts of the engine to be worn
Stroke. The distance the piston moves inside the cylinder.         beyond safe limits. TBO depends upon proper operation and
                                                                   maintenance in accordance with the engine manufacturer’s
Sump (aircraft engine component). A low point in an                recommendations. The overhaul of an engine when it
aircraft engine in which lubricating oil collects and is stored    reaches its TBO hours is not mandatory, except for certain
or transferred to an external oil tank. A removable sump           commercial operators that have the requirement written into
attached to the bottom of the crankcase of a reciprocating         their operations manual.
engine is often called an oil pan.
                                                                   TDC. Top dead center. The position of a piston in a

tank in which water and other contaminants collect and are         and the wrist pin, crankpin, and center of the crankshaft are
held until they can be drained out.                                all in line.

Supercharged engine. A reciprocating engine that uses a            TEL. Tetraethyl lead.
mechanically driven compressor to increase the air pressure
before it enters the engine cylinders.                             Test club. A wide-blade, short-diameter propeller used on a
                                                                   reciprocating engine when it is run in a test cell. A test club
Supercharger. An air compressor used to increase the               applies a specific load to the engine and forces the maximum
pressure of the air being taken into the cylinders of a            amount of air through the engine cooling fins.
reciprocating engine.
                                                                   Thermal efficiency. The ratio of the amount of useful work
Surface roughness. The condition of the surface of a               produced by a heat engine, to the amount of work that could
reciprocating engine cylinder wall that has been honed to          be done by all of the heat energy available in the fuel burned.
make it hold lubricating oil. Surface roughness is measured
in micro-inches rms.                                               Thermal expansion coefficient. A number that relates to
                                                                   the change in the physical dimensions of a material as the
Surge. A condition of unstable airflow, through the                temperature of the material changes. The thermal expansion
compressor of a gas turbine engine, in which the compressor        coefficient of aluminum is approximately twice that of steel.
blades have an excessive angle of attack. Surge usually affects
an entire stage of compression.                                    Thermal shock. The sudden change in engine operating
                                                                   temperature that occurs when engine power is suddenly
Synthetic oil. Oil made by chemical synthesis of a mineral,        reduced at the same time the airspeed, thus the cooling, is
animal, or vegetable base. Synthetic oils have appropriate         increased. Thermal shock occurs when an aircraft is required
additives that give them such characteristics as low volatility,   to rapidly descend to a lower altitude.
low pour point, high viscosity index, good lubricating
qualities, low coke and lacquer formation, and low foaming.        Thermistor. A semiconductor material whose electrical
                                                                   resistance varies with its temperature.
T
                                                                   Thermocouple. A device used to generate an electrical current.
Tachometer. An instrument that measures the rotational             A thermocouple is made of two dissimilar metal wires whose
speed of an object.                                                ends are welded together to form a loop. A voltage exists in
                                                                   the loop proportional to the difference in temperature of the
TAI. Thermal anti-ice. A system used to prevent the                junctions at which the wires are joined. The amount of current
formation of ice on an aircraft by flowing heated air inside       flowing in the loop is determined by the types of metals used
the structure.                                                     for the wires, the temperature difference between the junctions,
                                                                   and the resistance of the wires.
Tail pipe. The portion of the exhaust system of a gas turbine
engine through which the gases leave. The tail pipe is often       Thermosetting resin. A plastic resin that, once it has been
called the exhaust duct, or exhaust pipe.                          hardened by heat, cannot be softened by heating again.

TBO. Time between overhauls. A time period specified by            Thermostatic valve. A temperature-sensitive valve that
the manufacturer of an aircraft engine as the maximum length       controls the temperature of oil in an aircraft engine. When

the oil is cold, the valve shifts and directs the oil through      engine. The valves, pistons, and cylinders are overhauled,
the oil cooler.                                                    but the crankcase is not opened.

Thermoswitch. An electrical switch that closes a circuit           Torque. A force that produces or tries to produce rotation.
when it is exposed to a specified high temperature.
                                                                   Total pressure. The pressure a column of moving fluid
Three-dimensional cam. A drum-shaped cam in a hydro-               would have if it were stopped from its motion. Total pressure
mechanical fuel control whose outer surface is ground so that      is the sum of dynamic pressure and static pressure.
followers riding on the surface, as the cam is moved up and
down and rotated, can move mechanical linkages to control          Total temperature. The temperature of moving fluid that
the fuel according to a preprogrammed schedule.                    has been stopped from its motion. Total temperature is the
                                                                   sum of static temperature and the temperature rise caused by
Throttle. The control in an aircraft that regulates the power      the ram effect as the fluid was stopped.
or thrust the pilot wants the engine to produce.
                                                                   Townend ring. A type of ring cowling used over a single-row
Throw (crankshaft design). See offset throw.                       radial engine. The cross section of the ring is in the form of an
                                                                   airfoil that produces enough forward thrust to compensate for
Thrust horsepower. The horsepower equivalent of the thrust         the cooling drag of the engine. In the United States, townend
produced by a turbojet engine. Thrust horsepower is found by       rings are often called speed rings.
multiplying the net thrust of the engine, measured in pounds,
by the speed of the aircraft, measured in miles per hour, and      Track. The path followed by a blade segment of a propeller
then dividing this by 375.                                         or helicopter rotor in one rotation.

Thrust. The aerodynamic force produced by a propeller or           Tractor engine. An engine installed with the propeller facing
turbojet engine as it forces a mass of air to the rear, behind     the front of the aircraft. Thrust produced by the propeller
the aircraft. A propeller produces its thrust by accelerating      mounted on a tractor engine pulls the aircraft through the air.
a large mass of air by a relatively small amount. A turbojet       tractor propeller. A propeller mounted on an airplane in such
engine produces its thrust by accelerating a smaller mass of       a way that its thrust pulls the aircraft.
air by a much larger amount.
                                                                   Trailing edge. The thin edge at the rear of a propeller blade.
Time-Rite indicator. A patented piston-position indicator
used to find the position of the piston in the cylinder of a       Transducer. A device that changes energy from one form
reciprocating engine. The body of the Time-Rite indicator          to another. Commonly used transducers change mechanical
screws into a spark plug hole, and as the piston moves             movement or pressures into electrical signals.
outward in the cylinder, it contacts the arm of the indicator. A
pointer contacted by the arm moves across a calibrated scale       Transformer. An electrical component used to change the
to show the location of the piston in degrees of crankshaft        voltage and current in an AC circuit.
rotation before top center.
                                                                   Transonic range. Flight at Mach numbers between 0.8
Timing light. An indicator light used when timing magnetos         and 1.2. In this range, some air passing over the aircraft is
to an engine to indicate when the breaker points open. Some        subsonic, and some is supersonic.
timing lights incorporate an oscillator or buzzer that changes
its pitch when the points open.                                    Trend monitoring. A system for comparing engine
                                                                   performance parameters with a baseline of these same
TIT. Turbine inlet temperature. The temperature of the             parameters established when the engine was new or newly
gases from the combustion section of a gas turbine engine          overhauled. Parameters such as EGT, rpm, fuel flow, and oil
as they enter the turbine inlet guide vanes or the first stage     consumption are monitored on every flight, and the baseline
of the turbine.                                                    is plotted. Any deviation from a normal increase or decrease
                                                                   warns the technician of an impending problem.
Toggle. A T-shaped handle fitted onto the end of a cable
used to engage a simple starter with an overrunning clutch.        Tricresyl phosphate (TCP). A colorless, combustible
                                                                   compound, (CH3C6H4O)3PO, that is used as a plasticizer in
Top overhaul. An overhaul of the cylinders of an aircraft          aircraft dope and an additive in gasoline and lubricating oil.

TCP aids in scavenging lead deposits left in the cylinders        Turboshaft engine. A turbine engine in which several stages
when leaded fuel is burned.                                       of turbines are used to extract as much energy as possible.

TSFC. Thrust specific fuel consumption. A measure of              rotors, generators, or pumps.

of the number of pounds of fuel burned per hour for each          Turbosupercharger. A centrifugal air compressor driven by
pound of thrust produced.                                         exhaust gases flowing through a turbine. The compressed air
                                                                  is used to increase the power produced by a reciprocating
Turbine. A wheel fitted with vanes, or buckets, radiating         engine at altitude.
outward from its circumference. The reactive or aerodynamic
force caused by the fluid flowing through the vanes is            Two-spool engine. See dual-spool gas turbine engine.

                                                                  transformation that completes its operating cycle in two
Turbine engine. See gas turbine engine.                           strikes of the piston, one up and one down. When the piston
                                                                  moves up, fuel is pulled into the crankcase, and at the same
Turbine inlet guide vanes. A series of stator vanes               time the air/fuel mixture inside the cylinder is compressed.
immediately ahead of the first-stage turbine. The function of     When the piston is near the top of its stroke, a spark plug
the inlet guide vanes is to divert the hot gases in the proper    ignites the compressed air/fuel mixture, and the burning and
direction to enter the turbine, and to provide a series of        expanding gases force the piston down. Near the bottom of
convergent ducts which increase the velocity of the gases.        the stroke, the piston uncovers an exhaust port and the burned

Turbine nozzle. Another name for turbine inlet guide vanes.       down, it uncovers the intake port, and a fresh charge of fuel
                                                                  and air are forced from the crankcase into the cylinder.
Turbocharger. An exhaust-driven air compressor used to
increase the power of a reciprocating engine. A turbocharger      U
uses a small radial inflow turbine in the exhaust system to
drive a centrifugal-type air compressor on the turbine shaft.     UDF engine. Un-ducted Fan™. The trade name registered
The compressed air is directed into the engine cylinders to       by General Electric for a type of ultra-high-bypass turbofan
increase power.                                                   engine that drives one or more wide-blade propellers that have
                                                                  between eight and twelve blades. These blades, which are not
Turbo-compound engine. A reciprocating engine that has            enclosed in a duct or shroud, are very thin, have wide chords,
power recovery turbines in its exhaust system. The power          and are highly swept back in a scimitar shape that enables them
extracted from the exhaust by these turbines is directed into     to power airplanes flying in the speed range near Mach 0.8.
the engine crankshaft through a fluid coupling.
                                                                  UHB (ultra-high-bypass) engine. A turbine that drives a pair
Turbofan engine. A type of gas turbine engine that has a          of ducted or un-ducted contrarotating propellers which have
set of lengthened blades on the low-pressure compressor           eight to 12 variable-pitch blades. These blades are very thin,
or low-pressure turbine. Air moved by these special blades        have wide chords, and are swept back with a scimitar shape
bypasses the core engine and produces between 30% and             that allows them to power airplanes flying in the speed range
75% of the total thrust.                                          of Mach 0.8. The blades are made of advanced composites
                                                                  for high strength and light weight. USH engines may be of
Turbojet engine. A gas turbine engine that produces thrust by     either the tractor or pusher type, and have a bypass ratio in
accelerating the air flowing through it. A minimum of energy      excess of 30:1.
is extracted by the turbine, with the majority used to produce
an exhaust velocity much greater than the inlet velocity. The     Underspeed condition. A speed condition in which the
amount of thrust produced by the engine is determined by the      engine is turning at an RPM lower than that for which the
amount the air is accelerated as it flows through the engine.     propeller governor is set.

Turboprop engine. A turbine engine in which several stages        Unidirectional fibers. Fibers in a piece of composite material
of turbines are used to extract as much energy as possible. The   arranged so that they sustain loads in only one direction.
turbines drive reduction gears which in turn drive a propeller.
                                                                  Updraft carburetor. A carburetor that mounts on the bottom

of a reciprocating engine. Air entering the engine flows         Venture. A specially shaped restrictor in a tube designed to
upward through the carburetor.                                   speed up the flow of fluid passing through it. According to

Upper-deck pressure is the same as the turbocharger

V-blocks. A fixture that allows a shaft to be centered and

by an angle of between 45º and 90º. Pistons in two cylinders,

electrical current.                                              not parallel to the longitudinal axis of the aircraft.

Vapor lock. A condition of fuel starvation that can occur

between the tank and carburetor is heated enough for the         stiffness of the fluid, or its internal friction.

Vaporize. The changing of a liquid into a vapor.                 as temperature increases and contracts as temperature

Vectored-thrust engine. A turbojet or turbofan engine with       heats and decrease as it cools.
the fan and/or exhaust nozzles mounted in such a way that
they may be rotated in flight to produce forward, vertically     Volatile memory. Computer memory that is lost when the
upward, or rearward thrust.                                      power to the computer is turned off.

Velocity. A vector quantity that expresses both the speed        Volatility. The characteristic of a liquid that relates to its
an object is moving and the direction in which it is moving.     ability to vaporize or change into a gas.

Velocity turbine. A turbine driven by forces produced by the     Volumetric efficiency. The ratio of the volume of the charge
velocity, rather than the pressure, of gases flowing through     of the fuel and air inside the cylinder of a reciprocating engine
the vanes.                                                       to the total physical volume of the cylinder.


                                                                                                                           G-30

Von Ohain, Dr. Hans Pabst. The designer and developer              face. A worm gear is an irreversible mechanism. The rotation
of the first turbojet engine to power an airplane. His HeS3b       of the shaft, on which the worm gear locks the spur gear so
engine was built in Germany by the Heinkel Company and             its shaft cannot be rotated.
it flew in a Heinkel He178 airplane on August 27, 1939.
                                                                   Wrist pin. The hardened steel pin that attaches a piston to
Vortex. A whirling mass of air that sucks everything near it       the small end of a connecting rod.
toward its center.
                                                                   Y
Vortex dissipator. A high-velocity stream of compressor
bleed air blown from a nozzle into an area where vortices          Yaw. Rotation of an aircraft about its vertical axis.
are likely to form. Vortex dissipaters destroy the vortices that
would otherwise suck debris from the ground into engines           Z
mounted in pods that are low to the ground.
                                                                   Zero-lash valve lifter. A hydraulic valve lifter that maintains
W                                                                  zero clearance in the valve actuating mechanism.

Wake. The high-velocity stream of turbulent air behind an
operating aircraft engine.

Wankel engine. See rotating combustion (RC) engine.

Waste gate. A controllable butterfly valve in the exhaust pipe
of a reciprocating engine equipped with an exhaust-driven
turbocharger. When the waste gate is open, exhaust gases
leave the engine through the exhaust pipe, and when it is
closed, they leave through the turbine.

Watt. The basic unit of power in the metric system. One watt
is the amount of power needed to do one joule (0.7376 foot-
pound of work) in one second. One watt is 1⁄746 horsepower.

Wet-sump engine. An engine that carries its lubricating oil
supply in a reservoir that is part of the engine itself.

Wet-sump lubrication system. A lubrication system in
which the oil supply is carried within the engine itself. Return
oil drains into the oil reservoir by gravity.

Whittle, Sir Frank. The British Royal Air Force flying
officer who in 1929 filed a patent application for a turbojet
engine. Whittle’s engine first flew in a Gloster E.28 on May
15, 1941. The first jet flight in America was made on October
2, 1942, in a Bell XP-59A that was powered by two Whittle-
type General Electric I-A engines.

Windmilling propeller. A propeller that is rotated by air
flowing over the blades rather than powered by the engine.

Work. The product of a force times the distance the force
is moved.

Worm gear. A helical gear mounted on a shaft. The worm
meshes with a spur gear whose teeth are cut at an angle to its
